content.text: LLM pretraining is the initial large-scale training phase in which a transformer model learns to predict the next token in sequences drawn from massive text corpora. It is the most computationally expensive step in producing a language model — typically requiring thousands of GPUs running for weeks to months — but it is also the source of all the model's broad linguistic competence, factual knowledge, reasoning patterns, and coding ability. Every downstream capability, from instruction following to code generation to multi-step reasoning, depends on the quality of pretraining.
What It Is

Pretraining trains a model with randomly initialized parameters from scratch on an enormous corpus — typically 1 to 15 trillion tokens from web pages, books, academic papers, and code repositories. The training objective for the dominant architecture (the decoder-only transformer, used by GPT, LLaMA, Mistral, and similar models) is causal language modeling: at each position in the sequence, predict the next token given all previous tokens. This objective requires no labels — the text itself provides the supervision.

The model has no task-specific structure at this stage. Its only goal is to become a good predictor of natural language sequences. Because predicting the next word across diverse text domains requires understanding grammar, facts, reasoning, and context, the model implicitly develops powerful representations of language, knowledge, and structure. These representations are what make the pretrained model valuable as a starting point for subsequent fine-tuning stages.
Why It Matters

Pretraining establishes the capability ceiling for everything that comes after. Instruction tuning, RLHF, and task-specific fine-tuning can refine and redirect the model's behavior, but they cannot meaningfully add capabilities that pretraining failed to develop. A model pretrained primarily on low-quality web text will produce lower-quality reasoning even after extensive fine-tuning than a model pretrained on a high-quality diverse corpus.

Most practitioners never run full pretraining from scratch — it costs millions of dollars and requires specialized distributed training infrastructure. But understanding pretraining is essential for making informed decisions about which base models to use, how to evaluate them, whether to run domain-specific continued pretraining, and how to diagnose failure modes in fine-tuned models. The properties of the pretrained checkpoint propagate through every downstream stage.
Benefits
Universal language understanding: the model learns grammar, syntax, semantics, and pragmatics across multiple languages and registers.
Broad world knowledge: facts, events, concepts, and relationships from the training corpus are encoded in the model's parameters.
Transfer learning: the pretrained representations can be fine-tuned to perform new tasks with orders of magnitude fewer examples than training from scratch would require.
Emergent capabilities: at sufficient scale, capabilities like multi-step arithmetic, code generation, and chain-of-thought reasoning emerge without being explicitly trained.
No labels required: the training signal comes from the data itself, making it possible to use internet-scale text that would be prohibitively expensive to manually annotate.
Amortized cost: the expensive pretraining computation is done once; all downstream applications share the same pretrained checkpoint.
Trade-offs
Enormous compute cost: frontier model pretraining runs require thousands of H100/A100 GPUs for months, at costs ranging from tens of millions to hundreds of millions of dollars.
Knowledge cutoff: the model's knowledge is frozen at the end of pretraining data collection. Updating it requires either RAG, continued pretraining, or re-running pretraining.
Data quality sensitivity: low-quality, toxic, or biased training data is encoded in the model parameters and propagates to every downstream application.
No instruction following by default: a pretrained base model does not know how to respond helpfully to a user; it predicts continuations of text. Instruction tuning is required to make it useful.
Infrastructure requirements: distributed training across hundreds or thousands of GPUs requires specialized frameworks, custom checkpointing, and careful fault handling.
Irreproducibility: two pretraining runs on the same data with the same architecture will produce models with similar but not identical capabilities due to random initialization.
How It Works
Causal Language Modeling Objective
For a sequence of tokens
, the causal LM objective factorizes the joint probability autoregressively and minimizes the negative log-likelihood:

Every token in every sequence is a prediction target, unlike instruction tuning where only response tokens contribute to the loss. This means the model learns from 100% of the tokens in the dataset. Over trillions of such tokens, the model is rewarded for precisely predicting text across every domain in the corpus.
Scaling Laws
The Chinchilla scaling laws (Hoffmann et al., 2022) showed that the optimal ratio of training tokens
to model parameters
is approximately
. Given a fixed compute budget
(measured in FLOPs), the optimal model size and token count are:

In practice, this means that for a given budget, allocating half to model size and half to data volume produces the most capable model. The Llama family applied this principle: Llama-3-8B is trained on 15 trillion tokens rather than the ~160 billion tokens a strict Chinchilla-optimal run would prescribe, because the model will be used for inference many times and inference efficiency favors smaller, overtrained models.
The Training Loop

Each step of pretraining processes a batch of token sequences. The forward pass computes logits over the vocabulary at each token position using multi-head attention and feedforward layers. The cross-entropy loss is computed between the predicted distribution and the ground-truth next tokens. The backward pass computes gradients, and AdamW updates the model parameters. The learning rate follows a warmup-then-cosine-decay schedule over the full training run.
Architecture

The pretraining pipeline has three stages: data pipeline (web crawl → quality filtering → deduplication → tokenization → dataset blend), training loop (next-token prediction objective → forward pass through transformer layers → cross-entropy loss → backward pass with AdamW → distributed training), and base model output (language modeling capability → emergent capabilities → general knowledge → foundation for alignment).

attrs.alt: LLM pretraining architecture: data pipeline (web crawl, quality filter, dedup, tokenize, blend) feeds training loop (causal LM objective, transformer forward pass, cross-entropy loss, AdamW optimizer, distributed training), producing a base model with language modeling, emergent capabilities, and general knowledge

content.text: When to Use It

Full pretraining from scratch is almost never the right choice for application developers. It is appropriate only for organizations that need a model tailored to a specific language or domain for which no adequate pretrained model exists, or for research teams studying pretraining itself. The cost and infrastructure requirements make it prohibitive outside a small number of labs.

Continued pretraining is the more accessible form: starting from an existing pretrained checkpoint and training for additional steps on a domain-specific corpus. This is used to adapt a model to a new domain (medical text, legal documents, a low-resource language) without starting from scratch. Continued pretraining costs a fraction of full pretraining because the model already has most of the general language knowledge it needs — only the domain-specific representations need updating.

Continued pretraining is appropriate when: the domain vocabulary is not well represented in the base model (e.g., a base model trained primarily on English performing poorly on Korean text); the domain has highly specialized notation or structure (e.g., mathematical proofs, chemical formulas); or the deployment context requires the model to understand documents that differ structurally from the pretraining corpus.
How to Implement
Continued Pretraining

Continued pretraining follows the same procedure as pretraining but starts from an existing checkpoint. The key differences are: use a lower learning rate (typically 10x lower than the original pretraining peak) to avoid disrupting the general language knowledge already encoded; blend the domain data with a portion of general web text to maintain generalization; and use a short warmup period before reaching the peak learning rate.

# Continued pretraining on domain corpus using Hugging Face Trainer
from datasets import load_dataset
from transformers import (
    AutoTokenizer,
    AutoModelForCausalLM,
    DataCollatorForLanguageModeling,
    Trainer,
    TrainingArguments,
)
import torch

MODEL_ID = "meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B"  # existing pretrained base
DOMAIN_DATA = "data/domain_corpus.txt"  # domain-specific text

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_ID)
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    MODEL_ID,
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map="auto",
)

# Load and tokenize raw text — no labels needed, objective is CLM
def tokenize(examples):
    return tokenizer(
        examples["text"],
        truncation=True,
        max_length=2048,
        return_overflowing_tokens=True,  # split long docs into chunks
        stride=128,                        # overlap for context
    )

dataset = load_dataset("text", data_files={"train": DOMAIN_DATA})
tokenized = dataset["train"].map(tokenize, batched=True, remove_columns=["text"])

# CLM data collator: labels = input_ids (shifted internally by the model)
collator = DataCollatorForLanguageModeling(
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
    mlm=False,  # causal LM, not masked LM
)

# Lower LR than original pretraining to avoid catastrophic forgetting
training_args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="./checkpoints/domain-continued-pretrain",
    num_train_epochs=1,
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=8,
    learning_rate=1e-5,          # ~10x lower than typical pretraining peak
    lr_scheduler_type="cosine",
    warmup_steps=100,
    weight_decay=0.01,
    bf16=True,
    logging_steps=50,
    save_steps=500,
    report_to="none",
)

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    args=training_args,
    train_dataset=tokenized,
    data_collator=collator,
)

trainer.train()
trainer.save_model("./models/domain-base")
print("Domain-adapted base model saved")
Examples
Example 1 — Tokenization and Dataset Preparation

# Build a pretraining dataset from raw text files
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
from datasets import load_dataset
import itertools

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B")

# Load raw text
raw = load_dataset(
    "text",
    data_files={"train": "data/domain/**/*.txt"},  # all .txt files recursively
    split="train",
)

CHUNK_SIZE = 2048  # tokens per training example

def group_texts(examples):
    """Tokenize then chunk into fixed-length sequences for CLM."""
    tokenized = tokenizer(examples["text"], truncation=False)
    all_ids = list(itertools.chain(*tokenized["input_ids"]))
    # Drop the remainder so all chunks are full CHUNK_SIZE
    total = (len(all_ids) // CHUNK_SIZE) * CHUNK_SIZE
    chunks = [
        all_ids[i : i + CHUNK_SIZE]
        for i in range(0, total, CHUNK_SIZE)
    ]
    return {
        "input_ids": chunks,
        "attention_mask": [[1] * CHUNK_SIZE for _ in chunks],
    }

dataset = raw.map(group_texts, batched=True, num_proc=8, remove_columns=["text"])
print(f"Total training chunks: {len(dataset):,}")
print(f"Total tokens: {len(dataset) * CHUNK_SIZE:,}")
dataset.save_to_disk("data/pretrain_tokenized")
Example 2 — Evaluating a Base Model's Next-Token Prediction Quality

# Evaluate perplexity on held-out text — the standard pretraining quality metric
import torch
import math
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

def compute_perplexity(model_id: str, text: str) -> float:
    """
    Compute perplexity = exp(average cross-entropy loss) on the given text.
    Lower perplexity = better language modeling.
    """
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
        model_id, torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16, device_map="auto"
    )
    model.eval()

    encodings = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt").to(model.device)
    input_ids = encodings["input_ids"]
    MAX_LEN = 2048

    nlls = []
    for i in range(0, input_ids.size(1) - 1, MAX_LEN):
        chunk = input_ids[:, i : i + MAX_LEN].to(model.device)
        with torch.no_grad():
            outputs = model(chunk, labels=chunk)
        nlls.append(outputs.loss.item())

    ppl = math.exp(sum(nlls) / len(nlls))
    return ppl

# Lower perplexity on domain text after continued pretraining
with open("data/domain_holdout.txt") as f:
    domain_text = f.read()[:100_000]

base_ppl = compute_perplexity("meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B", domain_text)
domain_ppl = compute_perplexity("./models/domain-base", domain_text)

print(f"Base model perplexity: {base_ppl:.1f}")
print(f"Domain-adapted perplexity: {domain_ppl:.1f}")
print(f"Improvement: {(1 - domain_ppl / base_ppl) * 100:.1f}%")
Real-World Example

Meta's LLaMA-3-8B was pretrained on 15 trillion tokens from a blend of web text (CommonCrawl, filtered), code (GitHub), books, and academic papers. The training used 24,576 H100 GPUs running for approximately 77 days. The dataset blend was carefully calibrated: code was oversampled relative to its natural frequency because code-heavy pretraining improves structured reasoning and tool-use capabilities even in non-code tasks. Web text was filtered through multiple quality stages including perplexity filtering against a held-out reference dataset, language identification, and deduplication using MinHash LSH.

CodeLlama (Meta, 2023) demonstrates domain-specific continued pretraining at scale. The Llama-2-34B base model was further pretrained on 500 billion tokens of code from GitHub, reducing code perplexity by approximately 40% while maintaining most general-language capabilities. The continued pretraining used a learning rate of 2e-4 (lower than the original 3e-4) and a special fill-in-the-middle objective alongside standard CLM.
Common Mistakes
Treating base and instruct checkpoints interchangeably: base models do not follow instructions; prompting a base model with a question often produces a continuation rather than an answer.
Assuming the knowledge cutoff is the release date: pretraining data is collected months before the model is released. The actual knowledge cutoff may be 6-12 months earlier than the release date.
Underestimating data cleaning impact: models absorb toxic, biased, and factually incorrect content from the pretraining corpus. Downstream alignment cannot fully remove these biases.
Running continued pretraining without blending general data: fine-tuning only on domain data without mixing in general text tends to catastrophically degrade general language understanding.
Using too high a learning rate for continued pretraining: the original pretraining learning rate is appropriate for random initialization, not for adapting an already-trained model.
Skipping perplexity evaluation: perplexity on held-out domain text is the primary signal for whether continued pretraining is progressing correctly.
Best Practices
Invest heavily in data quality. The most impactful improvements in pretraining typically come from better data filtering and curation, not from architectural changes.
Deduplicate aggressively. Documents that appear multiple times in the training set are memorized rather than generalized, wasting training capacity.
Blend code into the corpus even for non-code applications. Code-heavy pretraining improves structured reasoning, JSON generation, and tool-calling behavior in all downstream applications.
Monitor loss curves closely. A sudden spike in training loss often indicates a corrupted batch or data format issue that must be diagnosed and corrected immediately.
Save checkpoints frequently during continued pretraining. If training degrades, the ability to roll back to an earlier checkpoint is critical.
Evaluate on downstream tasks at intermediate checkpoints to detect when continued pretraining has provided diminishing returns.
Performance and Scalability

Pretraining throughput is typically measured in tokens per second per GPU. A single A100-80GB processes approximately 15,000-25,000 tokens per second for a 7B model with sequence packing and flash attention. Across 1,000 GPUs, this yields roughly 15-25 billion tokens per GPU-day. Training a 7B model on 1 trillion tokens therefore requires approximately 40-70 GPU-days, or roughly 2-3 days on a 1,000-GPU cluster.

Distributed training uses three types of parallelism: data parallelism (the same model on multiple GPUs, each processing different batches), tensor parallelism (splitting individual weight matrices across GPUs for very large models), and pipeline parallelism (assigning different layers to different GPUs). For 7B models, data parallelism with ZeRO-3 sharding is typically sufficient. For 70B+ models, tensor and pipeline parallelism are necessary.

Training efficiency is the ratio of actual FLOPs to the theoretical peak FLOPs of the hardware. State-of-the-art runs achieve 35-50% model FLOPs utilization (MFU) — significantly below peak due to memory bandwidth bottlenecks, communication overhead, and overhead from gradient reduction. Flash attention and sequence packing are the two most impactful optimizations for improving throughput.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Megatron-LM (NVIDIA): the production framework used for training most frontier models, supporting tensor and pipeline parallelism, gradient checkpointing, and mixed precision.
DeepSpeed (Microsoft): ZeRO optimizer states, gradients, and parameters across devices to enable training models larger than single-GPU memory.
GPT-NeoX (EleutherAI): open-source pretraining framework used to train Pythia, Falcon, and other open models; based on Megatron-LM.
Flash Attention: hardware-aware attention implementation that reduces memory usage and increases throughput by 2-4x; essential for long-context pretraining.
Data preprocessing: CCNet, RedPajama, and Dolma are open-source pipelines for web crawl filtering, deduplication, and quality scoring at scale.
Evaluation: Eleuther AI Language Model Evaluation Harness (lm-evaluation-harness) is the standard benchmark suite for evaluating pretrained and instruction-tuned models.
Comparison with Alternatives

Security and Reliability

Pretraining data is the primary source of harmful content, biases, and misinformation in deployed models. Even with aggressive filtering, web-scale training corpora contain toxic language, demographic biases, copyrighted material, and private information. These are absorbed into the model's weights during pretraining and cannot be fully removed by downstream alignment — they can only be suppressed to varying degrees. Organizations building on top of pretrained models should understand what data the base model was trained on and what filtering was applied.

Memorization is a specific reliability risk: models memorize verbatim fragments of their training data, including personally identifiable information (PII), API keys, and other sensitive content that appeared in the pretraining corpus. Production systems should implement output filters that detect and block personally identifiable information and other sensitive content, and should assume that any data contained in a public model's training corpus may potentially be elicited by carefully crafted prompts.
Mental Model

Think of pretraining as building the brain. A freshly pretrained base model is like a person who has read an enormous library — they have absorbed vast knowledge, language facility, and reasoning patterns from everything they have read. But they have not yet been taught how to behave in a conversation, how to structure a helpful response, or how to decline inappropriate requests. They are knowledgeable but unaligned. Instruction tuning is the education that transforms this knowledgeable but unfocused reader into a responsive, helpful assistant. The quality of everything downstream — instruction following, tool use, reasoning — depends fundamentally on how much the model learned during this library-reading phase.
Key Takeaways
Pretraining establishes the capability ceiling: instruction tuning and fine-tuning refine behavior but cannot add capabilities that pretraining failed to develop.
The causal LM objective — predict the next token given all previous tokens — is the universal pretraining objective for decoder-only models and requires no labels.

Chinchilla scaling laws show the optimal compute allocation is roughly equal between model parameters and training tokens, which means many deployed models are intentionally overtrained for efficient inference.
Data quality and deduplication are the highest-leverage improvements available to pretraining practitioners — more impactful than architectural changes at equivalent compute.

Continued pretraining from an existing checkpoint is the accessible form of pretraining for domain adaptation — at a fraction of the compute and with a much lower risk of degrading general capabilities.
Pretrained models memorize training data, including PII and sensitive content; production systems must add output filtering and assume that adversarial prompts can elicit memorized content.
The pipeline is pretraining → instruction tuning → alignment; each stage addresses a different problem and cannot substitute for the others.
